summary: Uncle Bob recounts the architecture of a 1984 telephone voice response system (CDS) that he designed, describing its components: a RAM-based key-value database, field-labeled data format, and a finite state machine driven by a state transition table that invoked command-line processes. He then reframes these 30-year-old design choices using modern terminology—micro-services, message bus, BPEL engine, NoSQL, and JSON—to demonstrate that today's 'innovative' architectural patterns are essentially rediscoveries of older ideas. The post serves as a reminder that fundamental software architecture concepts remain constant even as terminology evolves.
key_points: The 1984 CDS system used a RAM-based key-value store (3DBB) fronted by a message-passing process—essentially a NoSQL database
Field Labeled Data (FLD) was a binary tree structure with JSON-like text encoding, predating modern data interchange formats
A finite state machine read from a configuration file and invoked command-line processes—an early form of BPM/BPEL orchestration
Multiple FSM processes ran simultaneously handling different phone lines, achieving concurrency through process isolation
Modern architectural buzzwords often describe patterns that have existed for decades under different names
money_quotes: For CDS I had a radical new idea.
Nowadays we'd call this JSON or XML. It had it's own text encoding scheme that, quite by accident, looked a lot like JSON.
The actions of the FSM were command lines.
We used a Micro-Service Architecture, communicating through a message buss, driven by Business Process Model (BPM) interpreted by a BPEL engine, backed by a NOSQL database holding values in JSON.
The more things change, the more they stay the same.
key_insight: Modern software architecture patterns like micro-services, message buses, and NoSQL databases are often rediscoveries of fundamental design principles that experienced programmers have been applying for decades.
